cards.back: It is an act of joint exploration.

cards.back: It sparks thoughts the participants never had before (starts in one place and ends in another).
cards.note: How to Know a Person - David Brooks

cards.front: What does it mean to treat attention as an "on/off switch" rather than a "dimmer" during conversation?
cards.back: It means treating presence as all-or-nothing: stopping other activities and being fully present rather than partially attending.

cards.note: How to Know a Person - David Brooks

cards.back: People struggle to picture or get excited about unfamiliar topics, preferring to talk about what they already know.
cards.note: How to Know a Person - David Brooks

cards.back: Find the thing the other person is most attached to (favoring familiarity over novelty).
cards.note: How to Know a Person - David Brooks

cards.front: What interviewing technique helps move a person from being a 'witness' to a storyteller ('author')?

cards.note: How to Know a Person - David Brooks

cards.note: How to Know a Person - David Brooks

cards.front: What does a good conversationalist explore regarding the relationship between the past and the present?
cards.back: How the person is experiencing their past events now (current perspective vs. initial reaction).
cards.note: How to Know a Person - David Brooks

cards.front: What is the psychological concept of "looping" in conversation?
cards.back: Repeating back what someone just said to ensure you accurately understood their meaning.
cards.note: How to Know a Person - David Brooks

cards.front: Why do conversation experts recommend the practice of "looping" despite its potential clumsiness?
cards.back: To check if you and speaker are on the same page
cards.note: How to Know a Person - David Brooks

cards.front: According to the 'Midwife Model', why does a midwife occasionally 'prod' the speaker?
cards.back: To encourage a deeper honesty in areas where the speaker is not fully honest with themselves

cards.note: Quantum computing for the very curious - Andy Matuschak

cards.front: In quantum computing, why can we ignore the global phase factor ($e^{ i\theta }$)?
cards.back: Because it doesn't change the measurement probabilities at the end of the circuit
cards.note: Quantum computing for the very curious - Andy Matuschak

cards.front: In quantum computing, why does the global phase factor not affect measurement probabilities?
cards.back: $|e^{ i\theta }|=1$ so it doesn't affect the probability
cards.note: Quantum computing for the very curious - Andy Matuschak

cards.front: Who can read encrypted data when using end-to-end encryption?
cards.note: Hacker Lexicon What Is End-to-End Encryption - Andy Greenberg

cards.front: Who can read encrypted data when using traditional encryption?
cards.back: The end user and whoever holds the encryption key (e.g. company server)
cards.note: Hacker Lexicon What Is End-to-End Encryption - Andy Greenberg

cards.front: Why is end-to-end encryption better than traditional encryption?
cards.back: Only the end users can see read the data. Messengers like company servers can't
cards.note: Hacker Lexicon What Is End-to-End Encryption - Andy Greenberg

cards.front: What can decrypt a message encrypted with your public key?
cards.note: Hacker Lexicon What Is End-to-End Encryption - Andy Greenberg

cards.front: What is the function of the public key in the lockbox analogy?
cards.back: People with your public key can put something in your lockbox and lock it (send and encrypt)
cards.note: Hacker Lexicon What Is End-to-End Encryption - Andy Greenberg

cards.front: What is the function of the private key in the lockbox analogy?
cards.back: Only you can unlock the lockbox to see the contents inside.
cards.note: Hacker Lexicon What Is End-to-End Encryption - Andy Greenberg

cards.front: What is the process of sending an encrypted message using public key encryption?
cards.back: 1.) Sender encrypt (sends) message with public key 2.) Recipient decrypts  (reads) message with private key
cards.note: Hacker Lexicon What Is End-to-End Encryption - Andy Greenberg

cards.front: What are the two core tenants of Liberalism?
cards.note: Liberalism A Political Ideology Explained - Jitender

cards.front: What is the "Rule of Law" in Liberalism?
cards.back: Individual and government subject to the same laws
cards.note: Liberalism A Political Ideology Explained - Jitender

cards.front: How does Liberalism relate economic and political freedom?
cards.back: Economic freedom is tied to political freedom
cards.note: Liberalism A Political Ideology Explained - Jitender

cards.front: What is one criticism of Liberalism relating limited government and inequality?
cards.back: The government can help combat inequality, but is too limited in Liberalism
cards.note: Liberalism A Political Ideology Explained - Jitender

cards.front: What is one criticism of Liberalism relating individual rights and inequality?
cards.back: Liberalism is too focused on individual rights and not widespread inequality (the common good)
cards.note: Liberalism A Political Ideology Explained - Jitender

cards.front: Why are children anti-fragile?
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: What are the two central claims in "The Anxious Generation"?
cards.back: Overprotection in the real world and underprotection in the virtual world
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: When did the "Great Rewiring" occur?
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: Why is "adolescents admitted to hospitals for self-harm" a better indicator than self-reports for determining rises in mental illness?
cards.back: self reports are influenced by factors like increases in general mental health awareness
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: According to "The Anxious Generation" what caused the first wave of the internet era?
cards.back: When personal computers and internet access became widespread
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: According to "The Anxious Generation" when was the first wave of the internet era?
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: According to "The Anxious Generation" what caused the second wave of the internet era?
cards.back: Ubiquity of smartphones and social media
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: According to "The Anxious Generation" when was the second wave of the internet era?
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: Why is social media on your smartphone more dangerous to mental health than social media on a PC?
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: Why is a low-stakes environment important for kids to learn?
cards.back: So they can safely try things out
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: What learning pattern do kids learn best from?
cards.back: Repetition with feedback in a low-stakes environment
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: Why do kids need unsupervised play with others?
cards.back: To teach them how to interact with others
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: What key social skills do kids learn from unsupervised play with others?
cards.back: How to interact with other people their age
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: Why are children motivated to learn key social skills (e.g. reading emotion) from unsupervised play with others?
cards.back: They want to be included in the group and "keep the fun going"
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: Typically how is online communication different from face-to-face interactions?
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: Why is asynchronous communication worse for children than synchronous?
cards.back: They don't learn key social skills
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: What features of social media makes them "conformity engines"?
cards.back: High exposure to what others are doing (posts) and numerical indicators of success / failure (likes)
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: How do kids learn using conformity?
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: Why is it dangerous for kids to conform to successful people on social media?
cards.back: What is successful on social media doesn't necessarily translate to real life
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: What does it mean when something is anti-fragile?
cards.back: It needs to go through hardship to become strong
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: What is "Defend Mode" (Anxious Generation)?
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: What is "Discover Mode" (Anxious Generation)?
cards.back: Seeing every experience as an opportunity
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: According to "The Anxious Generation", how safe should we keep kids?
cards.back: As safe as necessary, not as safe as possible
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: What does overprotecting children lead them to develop?
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: Why does overprotecting children lead to them developing anxiety disorders?
cards.back: they don't get the experiences necessary to build confidence. Kids are anti-fragile.
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: Why can social media be high-stakes?
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: According to The Anxious Generation, why is replacing free play with adult supervised activities bad?
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: Why did declining social cohesion affect childhood autonomy?
cards.back: Parents felt they couldn't trust their neighbors to look out for their kids
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: How did stranger danger affect parent's views of other adults in their community?
cards.back: They felt like they couldn't trust them with their kids
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: How did stranger danger affect parent's views of other's kids?
cards.back: They felt like they should stay away from other's kids
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: Define psychological safety?
cards.back: The idea that people can disagree with each other's ideas respectfully
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: How does psychological safety differ from emotional safety
cards.back: Psychological safety allows for negative emotions and criticism
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: What is "safetyism"?
cards.back: The idea that safety trumps everything, no matter how trivial the potential danger
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: How does overprotection make kids grow up in defense mode?
cards.back: Kids don't gain the confidence and skills needed to feel comfortable exploring the world
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: What is the unseen cost of kids spending hours online (social media, video games, etc.)?
cards.back: The opportunity cost of what they could have been doing
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: Heavy social media usage can affect attention in a similar way as [...] (disorder)
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: How can you slowly build a kid's confidence in themselves and their parent's trust in their independence at the same time?
cards.back: Let kids do something new without parental supervision, increasing the task magnitude slowly over time
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: What is the idea of "race to the bottom" for companies?
cards.back: "If I don't do this bad thing, someone else is gonna do this bad thing and I'll fall behind so therefore I must do this bad thing myself"
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: How is the internet a shallow version of the real world?
cards.back: It provides a false simulation of social connection, entertainment, learning, and sex satisfaction
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt

cards.front: Why is the virtual world an isolated version of the real world?
cards.back: You don't need to face the uncomfortableness of real life
cards.note: The Anxious Generation - Jonathan Haidt
